summary: Nadia Asparouhova examines the practical limitations of embryo selection technologies, arguing that media discourse overstates their current capabilities. She identifies three key constraints: IVF produces too few embryos (typically 3-5) for meaningful trait selection beyond essential disease screening, polygenic screening is statistically unlikely to reveal genetic outliers from such small samples, and polygenic risk scores themselves are unreliable models with wide error bars that vary across providers. While acknowledging the genuine value of genetic testing for serious monogenic diseases, she urges transparency about uncertainty and caution before making life-altering decisions based on speculative polygenic data. She concludes that the underlying goal of preventing genetic disease remains worthwhile, whether achieved through embryo selection, gene editing, or future technologies.
key_points: A single IVF cycle yields only 3-5 embryos, and after filtering for quality grade, sex, and even one genetic trait, viable options shrink dramatically—making 'designer baby' shopping among many traits practically impossible today.
Polygenic screening produces results clustered near the middle of a bell curve for any two parents, meaning with only a handful of embryos you're unlikely to find a meaningful genetic outlier.
Polygenic risk scores are statistical models, not hard-coded truths—they vary by provider, are often trained only on European ancestry data, and can give conflicting results for the same person.
Most current embryo screening is used for its clearest application: avoiding passing severe monogenic diseases like Huntington's or cystic fibrosis to children.
The broader ambition of preventing genetic disease may ultimately be better served by gene editing than embryo selection, bypassing the embryo quantity constraint entirely.
money_quotes: A world in which parents are 'shopping' among lots of different traits in their embryos simply doesn't exist today.
Finding a genetic outlier is like sticking your hand in a jar of green jelly beans and rummaging blindly around, hoping to grab the one red jelly bean: it's mostly wishful thinking.
Genomics researchers can't just look at our DNA, like some soothsayer blowing the dust off an ancient text, and tell us what they see.
A polygenic risk score is just a model that captures our best-guess thinking at the time. Like any model, it can have wide error bars.
It's another story to make life-altering choices for ourselves, or our children, based on highly speculative data, when the default choice might have been just fine.
If these services can't help customers understand what they should actually do differently, having access to these data insights might be more counterproductive than not having them at all.
Despite the panic about designer babies, most embryo screening today is being used for exactly this: avoiding passing severe, life-altering diseases on to one's children.
key_insight: Embryo selection is far more constrained than popular discourse suggests—limited embryo counts, statistical clustering, and unreliable polygenic models mean the technology is practically useful only for screening serious monogenic diseases, not designing children.
